name: Al-Hafiz
title: Al-Hafiz: The All-Preserving Guardian
meta_description: Al-Hafiz, the All-Preserving Guardian, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The One who holds the heavens and earth from falling without tiring, guards every soul from harm, and preserves every deed you ever did down to the last atom for the day you meet it. Qur'an verified.
keywords: Al-Hafiz, Al-Hafidh, The Preserver, The Protector, The Guardian, names of Allah, 99 names, Allah protects me, Allah records my deeds, kiram katibin, recording angels, Surah Hud 11:57, Ayat al-Kursi, Asma ul Husna, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, Tafsir as-Sa'di, and al-Tafsir al-Muyassar), in the voice of Buruja.
lede: Two fears sit on opposite ends of the same human heart, and most of us live somewhere between them. The first is the fear of losing what we love: that the people, the health, the small good life we have built could be taken in a moment, and there is nothing holding any of it in place. The second is the quieter fear that nothing we do really counts: that the kindness no one saw, the prayer no one witnessed, the wrong done to us in the dark, all of it simply evaporates, unweighed and unremembered. This name answers both fears at once.

Al-Hafiz, the All-Preserving Guardian. The One whose keeping holds the heavens and the earth in place so they do not slip from existence, and the One whose keeping records every word and deed of every soul so that not one atom of it is ever lost. He is both the hand that guards what you are afraid to lose, and the ledger that preserves what you are afraid will not matter. To know Him by this name is to learn that nothing real is ever truly in danger, and nothing you do is ever truly forgotten.

sections.h2: The name, and the root beneath it

ayah.arabic: فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُم مَّا أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ إِلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّي قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّونَهُ شَيْئًا ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ
ayah.text: But if you turn away, then I have already conveyed that with which I was sent to you. My Lord will give succession to a people other than you, and you will not harm Him at all. Indeed my Lord is, over all things, Guardian.

paras: Start with the word itself. Hafiz comes from three Arabic letters, ha, fa, za, the root of hifz, which gathers together a whole family of meanings the way no single English word can: to guard, to protect, to keep safe, to preserve, to retain, to commit to memory and never let slip. When you memorise the Qur'an you become a hafiz of it, one who holds it whole and lets nothing fall out. Stretch that same idea to the size of God and you begin to feel this name. Al-Hafiz is the One who holds all of creation, and everything that happens in it, so completely that nothing escapes Him and nothing is lost.

And the form the word takes here, hafiz, is built for permanence. Arabic has a pattern for traits that are intense, settled, and unfailing, qualities that belong to the one who carries them rather than coming and going. So this is not a God who guards now and then, or remembers some of it. Guarding and preserving are who He is.

Hear where it lands in Surah Hud. The prophet Hud has warned his people, and they have turned away, and he tells them plainly: you cannot harm Allah, my Lord is over all things Guardian. Ibn Kathir explains the close of the verse directly: Allah is Witness and Preserver of the speech of His servants and their deeds, and He requites them for it, if good then good and if evil then evil. So the very first thing the name does, in the very first verse, is to say two things at once. He preserves you, and He preserves the record of you. Al-Muyassar adds the protective edge Hud himself leaned on: He is the One who guards me from your reaching me with any harm. The man stood before a hostile people unafraid, because the One holding him was Al-Hafiz.

sections.h2: The keeping that holds the universe in place

ayah.arabic: اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَئُودُهُ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ
ayah.text: Allah - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Self-Sustaining. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them, and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High, the Most Great.

paras: Before this name guards anything small, understand the scale at which it works. In the greatest verse of the Qur'an, Ayat al-Kursi, the keeping of God is described in one breathtaking line: His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. The Arabic is wa la ya'uduhu hifzuhuma, the very word, hifz, the keeping of the two of them, the heavens and the earth.

Sit with what that is claiming. Every star that has not flung itself out of its orbit, every law of nature that held overnight while you slept, the whole structure of existence that simply did not collapse in the hours you were not watching, all of it is being actively held in place. It is not coasting on its own. Al-Sa'di, commenting on this verse, says that Allah has held the heavens and the earth from passing out of existence, and He does so without weariness and without fatigue. We exhaust ourselves holding up far less, a job, a household, a single worry through one sleepless night. The One who holds up everything, at every instant, is never once tired by it.

This is the floor under your feet that you never think about. The fear that everything is fragile, that it could all come apart, is true of you and false of Him. Nothing in the heavens or the earth is holding itself together. Al-Hafiz is holding it, and the effort that would crush any of us costs Him nothing at all.

sections.h2: The guardian over every single soul

ayah.arabic: إِن كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَّمَّا عَلَيْهَا حَافِظٌ
ayah.text: There is no soul but that it has over it a protector.

paras: Now bring it down to the size of one person, because the Qur'an insists this keeping reaches all the way to you. There is no soul, not one, in all the crowd of the living, except that it has a guardian set over it. Ibn Kathir explains this verse simply: over every soul there is from Allah a guardian who watches it and keeps it safe from harms. He then ties it to another verse, the one about the angels who come in turns, before a person and behind him, guarding him by the command of Allah.

Picture that for your own day. You moved through it thinking yourself unguarded, and around you the whole time was a keeping you could not see. The near miss you never knew was a near miss. The harm that was turned aside before it ever reached you. The nights you were unconscious and helpless for hours and woke up anyway, intact. None of that was luck. Al-Hafiz had set a guard over the soul, and the guard did not sleep when you slept.

Hold this beside the verse from Surah Hud. There Ibn Kathir noted that the misguided still went astray of their own choice, while by Allah's keeping and protection the believers were kept safe. So this guarding is not a cage that removes your freedom. You can still walk away from Him, as Hud's people did. But the one who turns toward Him discovers he was being held the entire time, carried through dangers he will only learn about on the day the whole record is opened.

ayah.text: And indeed, [appointed] over you are keepers,

paras: There is a second kind of keeping in this name, and for most of us it is the more searching one. Al-Hafiz does not only protect what happens to you. He preserves what comes out of you. Over you, the Qur'an says, are keepers, hafizin, noble and recording, who know whatever you do.

Ibn Kathir explains that these are angel-keepers who write down all of your deeds, and so, he warns, do not meet them with ugly acts. Al-Sa'di draws the circle even wider: they write your words and your deeds and they know them, and he adds that this includes the deeds of the hearts as much as the deeds of the limbs. Stop on that. Not only what your hands did and your tongue said, but what moved in your chest, the intention behind the smile, the grudge behind the polite word, the prayer no one heard you make. All of it is being preserved, by a keeping that never miscounts and never forgets.

Read one way, that is the most frightening sentence in the world. Read the right way, it is one of the most consoling. Because the same ledger that misses none of your sins also misses none of your unseen good. The kindness you did when no one was looking, the temptation you turned down in private, the tears you cried in an empty room, every one of them is written down by a noble scribe and kept perfectly for the day you will need it most. Ibn Kathir even relates a report that when the two keepers raise to Allah what they recorded in a day, and He sees the page opening and closing with the servant's seeking of forgiveness, Allah says: I have forgiven My servant what is between the two ends of the page. Nothing falls through. Not the worst of you, and never the best of you.

sections.h2: He preserves the deed, and He preserves its reward

ayah.arabic: وَمَا كَانَ لَهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّن سُلْطَانٍ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِالْآخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْهَا فِي شَكٍّ ۗ وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ
ayah.text: And he had over them no authority except [it was decreed] that We might make evident who believes in the Hereafter from who is thereof in doubt. And your Lord, over all things, is Guardian.

paras: If you want the whole of this name in a single line, al-Sa'di gives it to you here, commenting on the close of this verse in Surah Saba, where Allah says again, your Lord is over all things Guardian. He writes that Allah preserves the servants, and preserves over them their deeds, and preserves the recompense of those deeds, paying it to them complete and in full.

Read those three slowly, because together they answer the deepest fear underneath this name. He preserves the servants: you, your soul, kept and guarded through your whole life. He preserves your deeds: not one of them lost, not the wrong done to you and not the good you did unseen. And he preserves the recompense: the reward is not lost either, it is held in safekeeping until it is handed to you whole, kamilatan muwaffaratan, complete and undiminished, on the day you stand before Him.

This is why this name quietly dismantles the fear that nothing counts. We live as if good can leak away. As if the patience no one thanked us for, the right we never got back, the quiet faithfulness of years, might just dissolve into the air. Al-Hafiz says no. The deed is preserved, exactly as it happened, and the reward for it is preserved too, gaining nothing it did not earn and losing nothing it did. Al-Sa'di, on a related verse, puts it plainly: Allah records over them their deeds and requites them for the good of them and the evil of them. The God who forgets nothing is, for the believer, the best news there is.

sections.h2: The keeping you can hand your fear to

ayah.arabic: قَالَ هَلْ آمَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا كَمَا أَمِنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰ أَخِيهِ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَاللَّهُ خَيْرٌ حَافِظًا ۖ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ
ayah.text: He said, "Should I entrust you with him except [under coercion] as I entrusted you with his brother before? But Allah is the best guardian, and He is the most merciful of the merciful."

paras: There is a moment in the Qur'an where a grieving father teaches us what to do with this name. Yaqub, who has already lost his beloved son Yusuf to his other sons' treachery, is now being asked to send a second son away in their care. Every part of him is afraid. And listen to how he releases that fear: he does not say I will keep him, he says, Allah is the best guardian, fallahu khayrun hafizan, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.

Ibn Kathir captures the old man's heart here: Allah is the best in keeping, and He is the most merciful of the merciful, and Yaqub hoped that Allah would have mercy on his old age and his weakness and his longing for his son, and would return him to him. Notice what he did with the thing he could not protect. He had reached the absolute limit of his own keeping; he could not go with his son, could not guard the road, could not control his other sons' hearts. So he handed the keeping to the only One whose keeping never fails, and he named Him by this very name.

This is the practical gift of Al-Hafiz, and it is enormous. There is so much you love that you cannot protect. The child walking out the door. The parent getting older. The marriage, the health, the future you cannot see. You were never the real guardian of any of it; you only ever borrowed the role. So do what Yaqub did. Say it in your own words: Ya Hafiz, You are the best guardian, I am placing what I cannot keep into the keeping of the One who never loses anything. And then carry it more lightly, because the One now holding it is not tired, not asleep, and has never once dropped what was entrusted to Him.

sections.h2: Live as someone who is kept
paras: A name of Allah is never just information. It is meant to reshape the one who learns it, and Al-Hafiz reshapes you in at least three ways.

First, it gives you a deep and steady security. The believer who truly knows this name stops white-knuckling his own life. You are not the thread holding everything together; He is, and the keeping that holds the heavens does not strain at your small concerns. So you can sleep at night, literally, the way the verse promises, because the Guardian who keeps you does not sleep. You can release the loved one you cannot follow into the hands of the One who never lets go. The anxiety of holding it all yourself was always based on a misunderstanding: it was never on you to begin with.

Second, it teaches you watchfulness over yourself, what the scholars call muraqaba. If you genuinely believed that two noble scribes were preserving your every word and the very movements of your heart, the careless backbiting would die in your mouth and the private sin would lose its appeal. Not out of grim fear, but out of dignity: al-Sa'di says these are noble keepers, and it befits you to honour them. You would not litter in front of someone you respected. You stop littering your record because you finally believe it is being kept, and kept by the noble before the One who reads it.

Third, it makes your hidden goodness feel worth it again. So much of the good a person does is unseen and unthanked, and the temptation is to stop doing it, to conclude it does not count. Al-Hafiz is the cure for that quiet despair. Every unwitnessed kindness is witnessed. Every act of patience no one applauded is written, preserved, and its reward kept whole for you. You are not pouring your goodness into a void. You are making deposits with the One who loses nothing, and one day He will hand you back every last atom of it, complete.

Step back and let the two halves of this name come together. On one side, Al-Hafiz is holding the cosmos in place, the heavens and the earth kept from slipping into nothing, without the faintest fatigue, while He simultaneously sets a guard over the single soul walking through its ordinary, fragile day. On the other side, He is preserving the entire moral weight of every life, every word spoken and every intention hidden, the wrong suffered and the good done in secret, none of it lost, all of it kept perfectly for a day of perfect justice.

Put those together and you arrive at the quiet certainty this name was made to give: in the keeping of Al-Hafiz, nothing real is ever truly in danger, and nothing good is ever truly forgotten. The thing you are afraid to lose is held by One who does not drop what He holds. The good you are afraid does not matter is recorded by One who does not lose what He writes. And the reward you wonder if you will ever see is in safekeeping, complete and undiminished, waiting.

That is the mercy folded inside this name. Between the fear of losing everything and the fear that nothing counts, Al-Hafiz stands and answers both. He has kept the universe from the beginning, He is keeping you right now, and the record of who you really are is safe with Him, down to the last atom, for the day you finally see how nothing of it was ever lost.

dua.arabic: فَاللَّهُ خَيْرٌ حَافِظًا ۖ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ
dua.translit: Fallahu khayrun hafizan wa huwa arhamu ar-rahimin
dua.en: But Allah is the best guardian, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.

lessons.bold: Hand over what you cannot protect.
lessons.text: When Yaqub reached the limit of his own keeping, he said, Allah is the best guardian (12:64). Name the person or thing you are gripping too tightly and place it in the keeping of the One who never loses anything: Ya Hafiz, You are the best guardian.

lessons.bold: Sleep, because the Guardian does not.
lessons.text: Ayat al-Kursi says the preservation of the heavens and the earth never tires Him. Al-Sa'di notes He holds them from passing away without weariness. The keeping that carries the universe is not strained by your small concerns, so you can put the night down.

lessons.bold: Live as if your record is being kept, because it is.
lessons.text: Over you are noble scribes who write your words, deeds, and even the deeds of the heart (82:10-12). Let that awareness, says al-Sa'di's reading, raise you toward watchfulness: the backbiting dies in your mouth and the private sin loses its pull.

lessons.bold: Trust that your hidden good is not lost.
lessons.text: Al-Hafiz preserves the servant, the deed, and its reward, paying it complete and in full (al-Sa'di on 34:21). The kindness no one saw and the patience no one thanked are written down and kept whole for the day you need them most.

lessons.bold: Walk through your day as someone guarded.
lessons.text: Ibn Kathir explains that over every soul is a guardian from Allah who keeps it safe from harms (86:4, 13:11). The near misses you never knew about were a keeping you could not see. You were never moving through the day alone.

faq.q: What does the name Al-Hafiz mean?
faq.a: Al-Hafiz (حفيظ) means The All-Preserving Guardian, from the root h-f-z, which carries the linked senses of guarding, protecting, keeping safe, preserving, and committing to memory so that nothing is lost. Commenting on the name in Surah Hud 11:57 and Surah Saba 34:21, Ibn Kathir explains that Allah is Witness and Preserver of the speech and deeds of His servants and requites them for it. Al-Sa'di sums it up in three parts on 34:21: Allah preserves the servants, preserves over them their deeds, and preserves the recompense of those deeds, paying it complete and in full. So it means both that He protects you and that He keeps a perfect record of you.

faq.q: Is Al-Hafiz the same as the recording angels (kiram katibin)?
faq.a: The recording angels are how one half of this name reaches you. In Surah Al-Infitar (82:10-12) the Qur'an says, 'over you are keepers (hafizin), noble and recording, who know whatever you do.' Ibn Kathir identifies these as angel-keepers who write down all your deeds, and al-Sa'di notes that this includes the deeds of the heart as well as the limbs. The angels do the recording by Allah's command, but Allah Himself is Al-Hafiz, the One who ultimately preserves every deed and its reward. The angels keep the page; He keeps everything.

faq.q: Does Al-Hafiz mean Allah protects me, or that He records my deeds?
faq.a: Reading the verses together, the name carries both at once. There is the keeping of protection: 'there is no soul but that it has over it a protector' (86:4), which Ibn Kathir glosses as a guardian who keeps the soul safe from harms, and the verse Hud leaned on, 'my Lord is over all things Guardian' (11:57), where al-Muyassar notes He guards His messenger from harm. And there is the keeping of preservation: Allah records every word and deed (42:6, 82:10-12) and preserves its reward whole (34:21). Al-Hafiz is the One who both guards what happens to you and preserves what comes out of you.

faq.q: How is Al-Hafiz different from Al-Hafidh in the day-10 lesson of the 31-name track?
faq.a: They are easy to confuse, but in Buruja's 31-name track the day-10 session actually taught Al-Wali and An-Naseer, the Protecting Friend and the Helper, not this name. Al-Hafiz here is the distinct, canonical name of the 99: the Preserver who keeps and protects all things and records every deed. It is grounded in its own set of verses (11:57, 34:21, 42:6, 12:64, 86:4) and tafsir, and is built fresh as name number 38 in the traditional enumeration. If you studied Al-Wali and An-Naseer on day 10, this is a new and separate name to learn.

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We live between two fears: that we will lose what we love, and that the good we do will never really count. Al-Hafiz answers both with a single name. He is the One whose keeping holds the heavens and the earth from slipping out of existence without ever tiring, the One who sets a guard over every soul so that harms are turned aside you never even knew about, and the One who preserves every word, every deed, and every hidden movement of the heart, so that not one atom of who you really are is ever lost. And al-Sa'di reminds us He preserves not only the deed but its reward, paying it to you complete and in full. So you can do what Yaqub did at the edge of his grief, and hand the thing you cannot protect to the best of guardians. Nothing real is in danger in His keeping, and nothing good is forgotten.

O Allah, Al-Hafiz, the All-Preserving Guardian, You hold the heavens and the earth and they do not tire You, and You lose nothing of what we do. Guard us where we cannot guard ourselves, keep the ones we love whom we cannot follow, and preserve our hidden good until the day You hand it back to us whole. Make us watchful of the record being kept, and let us place all that we fear to lose into the keeping of the One who never lets go. Fallahu khayrun hafizan wa huwa arhamu ar-rahimin.

slides:
Al-Hafiz.

The All-Preserving Guardian.

We live between two fears: losing what we love, and the fear that the good we do never really counts.

This one name answers both.

First, the scale.

Ayat al-Kursi: 'His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not.' (2:255)

Every star in its orbit. Every law that held overnight. All of it actively held in place.

Al-Sa'di: Allah holds the heavens and the earth from passing out of existence, **without weariness and without fatigue.**

We exhaust ourselves holding up one worry through one sleepless night.

He holds up everything, and is never tired by it.

And it reaches all the way down to you.

'There is no soul but that it has over it a protector.' (86:4)

Ibn Kathir: a guardian who keeps the soul safe from harms. The near miss you never knew was a near miss.

There is a second kind of keeping. He preserves what comes **out** of you.

'Over you are keepers, noble and recording, who know whatever you do.' (82:10-12)

Al-Sa'di: including the deeds of the heart, not only the limbs.

Read one way, terrifying. Read right, it is mercy.

The same ledger that misses no sin misses **none of your unseen good.**

The kindness no one saw. The tears in an empty room. All written. All kept.

The whole name in one line, from al-Sa'di on 34:21:

Allah preserves the **servant**, preserves the **deed**, and preserves the **reward** of the deed, paying it complete and in full.

Nothing leaks away.

At the edge of his grief, Yaqub had to send away a second son.

He said: '**Allah is the best guardian**, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.' (12:64)

He handed what he could not protect to the One who never loses anything.

There is so much you love that you cannot protect.

So do what Yaqub did.

Ya Hafiz, You are the best guardian. I place what I cannot keep into the keeping of the One who never lets go. 🤍
social.caption:
Al-Hafiz, the All-Preserving Guardian, answers two fears at once: that we will lose what we love, and that the good we do will never count.

Grounded in classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, al-Sa'di, al-Muyassar), this name has two wings. He is the keeping that holds the heavens and the earth from slipping out of existence, and, as Ayat al-Kursi says, their preservation never tires Him (2:255). Al-Sa'di: He holds them from passing away without weariness and without fatigue. And that same keeping reaches down to one soul: 'there is no soul but that it has over it a protector' (86:4), a guardian, says Ibn Kathir, who keeps it safe from harms you never even saw coming.

The second wing is just as tender. He preserves what comes out of you: 'over you are keepers, noble and recording, who know whatever you do' (82:10-12), including, al-Sa'di notes, the deeds of the heart. The same record that misses no sin misses none of your unseen good. And al-Sa'di gives the whole name in one line on 34:21: Allah preserves the servant, preserves the deed, and preserves its reward, paying it complete and in full.

This is why Yaqub, at the edge of his grief, could send away a second son and say, 'Allah is the best guardian' (12:64). He handed what he could not protect to the One who never loses anything.

What is the thing you love that you cannot protect? Place it in His keeping. 🤍

email.subject: The One who loses nothing
email.alt_subject: For what you love but cannot protect
email.preview: Al-Hafiz, the All-Preserving Guardian. He holds the heavens without tiring, guards your soul from harms you never see, and keeps every good deed whole.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Two fears sit on opposite ends of the same heart. The first is the fear of losing what we love: the people, the health, the small good life we have built, all of it feeling like it could be taken in a moment with nothing holding it in place. The second is quieter: the fear that nothing we do really counts, that the kindness no one saw and the wrong done to us in the dark simply evaporate, unweighed and unremembered.

One of the names of Allah answers both at once: Al-Hafiz, the All-Preserving Guardian.

Start with the scale of it. In the greatest verse of the Qur'an, Ayat al-Kursi, the keeping of God is described in one line: 'His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not' (Al-Baqarah 2:255). Commenting on this, the scholar al-Sa'di says that Allah holds the heavens and the earth from passing out of existence, and He does it without weariness and without fatigue. We wear ourselves out holding up far less, one worry through one sleepless night. The One who holds up everything, at every instant, is never once tired by it.

And that same keeping reaches all the way down to you. 'There is no soul but that it has over it a protector' (At-Tariq 86:4). Ibn Kathir explains this as a guardian from Allah who keeps the soul safe from harms. The near miss you never knew was a near miss. The nights you were unconscious for hours and woke up anyway. That was not luck; it was a keeping you could not see.

There is a second kind of keeping in this name, and it is just as tender. Al-Hafiz preserves not only what happens to you, but what comes out of you. 'Over you are keepers, noble and recording, who know whatever you do' (Al-Infitar 82:10-12). Ibn Kathir says these angels write down all your deeds, and al-Sa'di adds that this includes the deeds of the heart, not only the limbs. Read one way that is frightening. Read the right way it is a mercy, because the same ledger that misses none of your sins also misses none of your unseen good: the kindness no one applauded, the tears cried in an empty room, all written and kept whole.

Al-Sa'di gives the entire name in a single line, commenting on Surah Saba 34:21: Allah preserves the servants, preserves over them their deeds, and preserves the recompense of those deeds, paying it to them complete and in full. The deed is not lost. The reward is not lost. Both are kept in safekeeping until the day they are handed to you whole.

This is why a grieving Yaqub, asked to send away a second son after losing Yusuf, could let go of his fear with these words: 'Allah is the best guardian, and He is the most merciful of the merciful' (Yusuf 12:64). He had reached the limit of his own keeping. So he handed what he could not protect to the only One whose keeping never fails, and named Him by this very name.

There is so much you love that you cannot protect, and so much good you do that no one sees. Al-Hafiz answers both. Say it in your own words: Ya Hafiz, You are the best guardian, I place what I cannot keep into the keeping of the One who never lets go. Nothing real is in danger in His keeping, and nothing good is ever forgotten.

Fallahu khayrun hafizan wa huwa arhamu ar-rahimin.
